docs.title: Tactical OSINT
docs.text:
Open-source intelligence, structured for operators.

A public knowledge base covering OSINT methodology, geospatial intelligence, media verification, and adversarial threat analysis. Built for analysts, researchers, and anyone who needs to find signal in noise.

Get Started Browse Tools

docs.title: What's Inside
docs.text:
• OSINT

    Search operators, social media recon, username and email enumeration, and open-source collection methodology.

    Start here

  • GEOINT

    Imagery analysis, geolocation techniques, satellite source directories, and map-based intelligence workflows.

    Geospatial

  • Verification

    Image and video forensics, reverse search pipelines, source credibility frameworks, and disinformation detection.

    Verify

  • Adversarial Analysis

    Threat actor profiling, infrastructure pivoting, disinformation campaign mapping, and network analysis.

    Analyze

Operational Security

All techniques documented here are for lawful, ethical intelligence research only. Always operate within applicable legal frameworks. Know your jurisdiction. Protect your sources and methods.

docs.title: Adversarial Analysis
docs.text: Adversarial analysis focuses on understanding the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of threat actors — from state-sponsored groups to influence operations. This section bridges OSINT methodology with structured threat intelligence.

docs.title: GEOINT
docs.text: Geospatial intelligence (GEOINT) involves the analysis of imagery, geospatial data, and mapping tools to derive location-based intelligence. This section covers techniques from geolocating a single image to analyzing patterns across satellite imagery.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: The technical skills are learnable in weeks. The analytical discipline takes years. This section covers the foundational concepts, frameworks, and mental models that separate systematic intelligence collection from random googling.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Topic Description What is OSINT What open-source intelligence actually means in 2025

docs.title: What is OSINT
docs.text:
The textbook definition is useless. "Information collected from publicly available sources" describes everything from reading a newspaper to running automated infrastructure scans. It tells you nothing about how OSINT actually works or why it matters.

Here's a better framing: OSINT is the discipline of turning fragmented, publicly accessible data into structured, actionable intelligence — under time pressure, without burning sources, and with enough rigor that your conclusions can be defended.

That's the job.

docs.title: Watch It Happen in Real Time
docs.text:
A video surfaces on Telegram at 03:17 UTC. Forty seconds of shaky footage. Someone claims it shows a strike on a fuel depot in a conflict zone. The post gets 4,000 shares in under an hour.

The video might be real. It might be six months old. It might be from a different country entirely. You have no way to know — yet.

By 05:40 UTC, a team working open sources has established the following:

The location is not what the post claims. A rooftop water tank visible at 0:23, a distinctive antenna cluster, and the angle of a highway interchange place the footage in a city 340 kilometers from where the caption says it occurred. Google Earth imagery confirms the intersection. Sentinel-2 satellite data from the same week shows no thermal anomaly at the claimed site. There was no strike.

The original account posting the video was created 11 days ago. Its prior posts are a mix of legitimate conflict footage and two other videos later identified as misattributed. The account shows coordinated amplification patterns — dozens of repost accounts active within minutes of each post, several sharing the same profile photo metadata fingerprint.

This is OSINT. Not a single piece of that analysis required a paid database, a leaked file, or a source inside any organization. It required knowing where to look, what to look for, and how to connect what you find.

docs.title: What "Open Source" Actually Covers
docs.text:
The word "open" is misleading. It implies easy, obvious, freely given. Most useful OSINT data is technically public but practically obscure — buried in corporate registries, embedded in image metadata, indexed in breach repositories, logged in passive DNS records, or visible only if you know the right satellite pass time.

graph TD
    A[Surface Web] -->|Search engines, news, gov sites| I[Collection]
    B[Social Platforms] -->|Telegram, X, LinkedIn, forums| I
    C[Specialized Databases] -->|Shodan, WHOIS, flight/ship AIS| I
    D[Geospatial Sources] -->|Satellite imagery, Street View, mapping APIs| I
    E[Document Repositories] -->|Court records, company filings, patents| I
    F[Breach & Leak Data] -->|Indexed breach repositories, paste sites| I
    I --> J[Processing & Triage]
    J --> K[All-Source Analysis]
    K --> L[Intelligence Product]

    style A fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style B fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style C fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style D fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style E fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style F fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style I fill:#111111,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style J fill:#111111,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style K fill:#111111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style L fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000

The sources that require the most skill to exploit are rarely the exotic ones. A corporate WHOIS record or a shipping manifest can break an investigation that months of social media monitoring couldn't. The discipline is knowing which layer of the source hierarchy to hit first for a given question.

docs.title: From Raw Data to Intelligence
docs.text:
Data is not intelligence. A list of IP addresses is not intelligence. A folder of screenshots is not intelligence. Intelligence is data that has been collected, evaluated for reliability, processed for meaning, and analyzed in context — then communicated in a form that supports a decision.

flowchart LR
    A([Raw Data]) --> B[Collection\nIdentify relevant sources\nand gather systematically]
    B --> C[Processing\nFilter noise, normalize\nformat, tag sources]
    C --> D[Analysis\nFind patterns, gaps,\ncontradictions, pivots]
    D --> E[Production\nStructured output\ntied to a requirement]
    E --> F([Actionable\nIntelligence])

    style A fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#aaa
    style B fill:#111,stroke:#333,color:#e0e0e0
    style C fill:#111,stroke:#333,color:#e0e0e0
    style D fill:#111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style E fill:#111,stroke:#333,color:#e0e0e0
    style F fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000

The gap between collection and production is where most OSINT efforts fail. Analysts collect aggressively and analyze poorly. They produce timelines when they should produce assessments. They report what they found instead of what it means.

The Telegram video example above didn't stop at "the video is mislocated." It produced an assessment: the video is not what it claims, the account is likely part of a coordinated inauthentic operation, and amplification began before most humans in the region would have been awake. That's actionable. A timeline of screenshots is not.

docs.title: How Sources Converge on a Target
docs.text:
No single source is sufficient. The strength of OSINT is triangulation — multiple independent data streams converging on the same conclusion. When satellite imagery, social media metadata, corporate registry data, and passive DNS records all point to the same entity, that convergence is evidence. Any one of them alone is a lead.

graph TD
    S1[Satellite Imagery\nno thermal anomaly] --> V{Verification\nAssessment}
    S2[Social Metadata\naccount age: 11 days] --> V
    S3[Visual Geolocation\n340km from claimed site] --> V
    S4[Network Analysis\ncoordinated amplification] --> V
    S5[Historical Footage\nprior misattributions] --> V
    V --> C1[Video is fabricated\nor misattributed]
    V --> C2[Account is inauthentic]
    V --> C3[Operation is coordinated]

    style S1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style S2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style S3 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style S4 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style S5 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style V fill:#111111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style C1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#ff6b35
    style C2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#ff6b35
    style C3 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#ff6b35

This is the method. Build the source set, work each strand independently, let the convergence drive the conclusion. If your sources aren't converging, you don't have an answer yet — you have a hypothesis. Those are different things, and conflating them is how bad analysis gets published.

docs.title: What OSINT Is Not
docs.text:
OSINT is not hacking. Accessing systems without authorization is illegal regardless of whether the data inside is "publicly interesting." Scraping past a CAPTCHAs with automation tools operates in a legal grey zone that varies by jurisdiction. Know the line.

OSINT is not journalism, though journalists do it. It's not threat intelligence, though threat analysts use it. It's not investigation, though investigators rely on it. It's a discipline and a methodology that sits underneath all of those fields. The tradecraft transfers. The professional obligations of each field do not.

Know your jurisdiction

The legality of specific OSINT techniques — particularly automated collection, breach data access, and social media scraping — varies by country and changes frequently. This site documents techniques for educational purposes. You are responsible for understanding and complying with applicable law.

For daily OSINT analysis, techniques, and breakdowns of live investigations, follow @tactical_osint on Telegram.

docs.title: OSINT
docs.text: Open-source intelligence (OSINT) is the collection and analysis of information from publicly available sources. This section covers systematic methodology for finding, validating, and structuring open-source data.

docs.title: Reports
docs.text: Structured intelligence assessments produced by Iron Compass, HexDefender's in-house analysis system. Every report here is built on a documented doctrine: sources are vetted and graded before a claim is used, confidence labels are computed rather than asserted, and every quantitative claim shows its inputs. Browse by category below, or filter the full archive by tag.

docs.title: Russia's July 2026 Kyiv Strike: Degradation Campaign or Coercive Escalation?
docs.text: Download PDF

docs.title: 1. Abstract / Bottom Line
docs.text: Russia conducted one of the largest missile-and-drone strike packages of the war against Kyiv on the night of July 1-2, 2026, in a strike its Ministry of Defense characterized as retaliation for Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil infrastructure. It is likely (Confidence: Likely; see Confidence Ledger, Section 7) that this strike represents continuation and intensification of a sustained degradation campaign against Ukrainian energy and military-industrial infrastructure -- Escalation Rung 3 (Degradation) -- rather than a one-off proportionate reprisal or the opening move of a theater expansion. This assessment is made with a seriously argued alternative still live: the strike's record ballistic-missile density is disconfirming evidence that the action may instead represent a shift toward coercive signaling that exceeds simple retaliatory proportionality (Section 6). Separately, and at high confidence, Russia's ground offensive gained territory in June 2026 at roughly 6% of its June 2025 pace (Section 5), consistent with a campaign under sustained attritional strain even as its strike campaign against Ukrainian infrastructure continues.

docs.title: 2. Methodology Note
docs.text: This report applies: the Source Vetting Protocol (per-claim vetting gate, run before any grading or citation); the Source Reliability Framework (source-level grading); the Citation Standard (mandatory inline citation format); the Confidence Assessment Doctrine, including its Part VIII vetting precondition (per-claim confidence scoring); the Quantitative Methods document's Confidence Scoring Function, Territorial Momentum calculation, and Cost Exchange Ratio (shown-work quantitative analysis); the Analysis Doctrine's Hypothesis Engine (Part VIII) and Escalation Ladder model (Part XIII); the Escalation Doctrine (rung assignment) and its Quantitative Methods Escalation Index formalization; the Forecasting Doctrine (scenario tree); and the Writing Doctrine's Part IX Thesis Report Structure governing this document's own shape. Every claim below completed the sequence Vet -> Grade -> Cite -> Assess before appearing in this report.

docs.title: 3. Source Vetting Summary
docs.text:
Fifteen candidate sources were considered for this report. Nine passed vetting cleanly (PASS). Five were usable only with specific, disclosed caveats (FLAG). One was excluded outright (REJECT). Two flagged candidates (C-11, C-12) were ultimately not separately used in the report body -- C-11 was merged into C-10 as a duplicate report of the same statement, and C-12 was superseded by higher-quality corroborated alternatives already in the report.

Outcome Count Candidate IDs PASS 9 C-01, C-02, C-03, C-04, C-05, C-06, C-07, C-09, C-15 FLAG 5 C-08 (incentive disclosed), C-10 (incentive disclosed), C-11 (merged duplicate of C-10), C-12 (no profile, single-source, unused), C-14 (aggregated estimate) REJECT 1 C-13 (sensationalized language, no traceable original, no unique checkable fact)

Rejected source detail: A tabloid-style claim characterizing the strike using unquantified magnitude and loaded language ("massive," "inferno") was encountered during collection. It was rejected under the Source Vetting Protocol's Rejection Criteria 1 and 4 (Part V of that document): no specific, dated original article could be traced and independently confirmed during this vetting pass, and once the magnitude/loaded language is stripped away, the claim carried no checkable fact beyond what Tier 1-3 sources (C-01 through C-07) had already independently established with actual figures. This mirrors the exact sourcing failure that motivated the Source Vetting Protocol's creation: a sensational claim that would previously have been dropped into a report as a bracketed footnote next to a wire report, with no distinction drawn between the two.

Notable disclosed-incentive sources still used: Russia's Ministry of Defense (C-08) and Ukrainian President Zelensky (C-11) are both cited for the specific fact that they made specific public statements -- not adopted as independent confirmation of the substance of those statements. See the full Vetting Ledger (Section 10) for every candidate's complete checklist record.

docs.title: 4.1 The July 1-2, 2026 Strike on Kyiv -- Scale
docs.text:
Russian forces launched approximately 570 munitions at Ukraine overnight -- 74 missiles (including 28 ballistic missiles, described as a record number for a single strike on the capital) and 496 long-range strike drones and decoys -- primarily targeting Kyiv [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass], corroborated by independent reporting on the same missile/drone breakdown [CNBC, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass -- note: this source's own headline used the unquantified term "massive" to describe the strike; that word is not carried into this report, per the Source Vetting Protocol's Item 3 bias/sensationalism screen, which applies regardless of the citing source's general reliability grade]. Ukrainian air defense reported intercepting or suppressing 48 of the missiles and 476 of the drones; 25 ballistic missiles and approximately 12 drones struck an estimated 33 locations across the city [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass].

At least 20 buildings were struck directly, with damage recorded at nearly 100 buildings in total across Kyiv [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass], consistent with independent reporting of damage across more than 30 sites including residential apartment blocks, a hotel, a scientific research institute, and an ambulance station [Al Jazeera, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass].

docs.title: 4.2 Casualties -- A Rising, Not Simply Conflicting, Toll
docs.text:
Casualty figures for this strike varied significantly across sources reporting at different points in the 48 hours after the strike:

Reported toll Source Reporting time 17 killed, 90+ injured Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko [Tier 1, Reliability B, Independence Medium, Vetting: Pass] Morning of July 2 At least 21 killed PBS NewsHour [Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass] July 2 At least 22 killed Al Jazeera [Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass] July 2 At least 25 killed, 80+ injured Ukraine State Emergency Service [Tier 1, Reliability B, Independence Medium, Vetting: Pass] July 2 (later) At least 27 killed, 91 injured ISW [Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass] July 2 (assessment) At least 30 killed, near 90 injured Kyiv Independent [Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence Medium, Vetting: Pass] July 2-3 (updated)

Per the Collection Doctrine's Contradiction Resolution Framework (Step 2: check for a common upstream explanation before treating a spread as a true factual conflict), this spread is not treated as a Confirmed-vs-False contradiction between disagreeing sources. All six figures are point-in-time snapshots during an active search-and-rescue operation following a mass-casualty strike, reported within a 48-hour window in ascending order consistent with an evolving, rising confirmed toll rather than genuinely inconsistent counts. This is labeled Likely: at least 25-30 killed, rising from an initial confirmed count of 17, rather than Conflicting, per the Collection Doctrine's instruction that volume/spread alone does not establish a true contradiction once a plausible common explanation exists -- but every individual cited figure above still carries its own citation and reporting timestamp rather than being silently collapsed into one number, since a reader auditing this claim needs to see the actual reporting sequence, not just Iron Compass's interpretation of it.

docs.title: 4.3 Stated Rationale and Target Characterization
docs.text:
Russia's Ministry of Defense stated the strike was carried out in response to Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil facilities that have caused fuel shortages, and characterized it as a "massive attack" targeting military-industrial facilities and fuel/energy complexes [Russian Ministry of Defense statement, Tier 1, Reliability C, Independence Low, Vetting: Flag -- direct interest in characterizing its own strike as proportionate retaliation against military/industrial targets; the term "massive" is reported here only as a direct quote of the source's own characterization, not adopted as Iron Compass's descriptive language, per the Escalation Doctrine's Part II Rule 1 discipline of checking self-framing against indicators rather than accepting it].

Ahead of the strike, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky publicly warned of an impending "massive Russian strike" [Zelensky public statement, Tier 1, Reliability B, Independence Low-Medium, Vetting: Flag -- head of state of the affected country has a plausible interest in emphasizing threat severity; cited here only for the fact and timing of the warning, not as independent confirmation of the strike's eventual scale]. The subsequent warning is credited with contributing to a lower casualty toll than the strike's physical scale might otherwise have produced [Al Jazeera, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass].

docs.title: 4.4 Broader Campaign Context -- Russian Ground Offensive Momentum
docs.text: Independent of the July 2 strike, Russia's spring-summer 2026 ground offensive has not achieved operationally significant gains: Russian forces' rate of territorial advance in June 2026 was a small fraction of the rate achieved in June 2025 [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass]. This claim is developed with full shown arithmetic in Section 5.

docs.title: 4.5 Ukraine's Counter-Strategy
docs.text: Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian oil facilities and rear-area logistics -- the action Russia's Ministry of Defense cited as the strike's trigger (Section 4.3) -- has been assessed by Western analysts as bringing a significant shift in the war's dynamics [NPR, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass]. The European Union has transferred an additional $4.44 billion of a $101 billion loan facility toward Ukrainian drone technology [CNBC, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass -- this figure describes an EU financing action; no Tier 1 EU statement was independently located and vetted for this report, so it is carried at Tier 3 sourcing only, per Section 4's ordinary citation rules].

docs.title: 5.1 Confidence Score -- "At least 20 buildings struck directly"
docs.text:
Per the Quantitative Methods document, Part I:

Evidence Strength: Strong (specific count, internally consistent,
                    corroborated) -> E = 3
Reliability: A (ISW)                -> R = 3
BaseScore = (0.6 x 3) + (0.4 x 3) = 3.0  -> Base Bucket = High (5)

Corroboration: n = 1 independent source (Al Jazeera, Reliability B,
               reporting damage across 30+ related sites) -> already
               at cap, no further step needed.

Ceilings: n >= 1, no single-source ceiling; no unresolved contradiction
on this specific fact; source not F-provisional; not a causal claim;
evidence is within the Breaking/Current freshness window.

FinalBucket = 5 (High) -> Lexicon: Almost certain / Very likely

Reported as: Almost certain that at least 20 buildings in Kyiv were struck directly (BaseScore 3.0 -> High; n=1 independent corroboration; no ceilings triggered).

docs.title: 5.2 Confidence Score -- "Russian June 2026 territorial gains, single source"
docs.text:
Evidence Strength: Strong (specific figure, methodologically
                    consistent with the source's own established
                    tracking method) -> E = 3
Reliability: A (ISW)                  -> R = 3
BaseScore = 3.0 -> Base Bucket = High (5)

Corroboration: n = 0 (no second independently-tracked territorial
               dataset was vetted for this report)

Ceiling: Single-source ceiling (Confidence Assessment Doctrine, Part
         IV, Rule 1) applies regardless of Reliability A and Strong
         evidence -> capped at Moderate-High (4), never High, because
         reaching the top of the scale requires independent
         corroboration by definition, not source quality alone.

FinalBucket = 4 (Moderate-High) -> Lexicon: Likely

Reported as: Likely (not Almost Certain) that Russian territorial gains in June 2026 were approximately 30.42 km² -- capped below High by the single-source ceiling even though the source is A-grade with Strong evidence, per the Confidence Assessment Doctrine's explicit rule that source quality alone cannot substitute for corroboration. This is a direct, deliberate illustration of Confidence Assessment Doctrine Part IV, Rule 1 in operation.

docs.title: 5.3 Territorial Momentum
docs.text:
Per the Quantitative Methods document, Part V, using ISW's (Tier 2, Reliability A) reporting [Vetting: Pass -- see Section 5.2 for the confidence treatment of this same dataset]:

NetChange(June 2026) = 30.42 km² gained
T = 1 month
Rate(June 2026) = 30.42 / 30 days = 1.01 km²/day

Comparison:
Rate(June 2025) = 481.25 km² / 30 days = 16.04 km²/day

Rate ratio: 1.01 / 16.04 ≈ 0.063  -- June 2026's pace of advance was
approximately 6.3% of June 2025's pace over the same calendar month.

H1 2026 total: 622.60 km²
H1 2025 total: 2,189.87 km²
H1 ratio: 622.60 / 2,189.87 ≈ 28.4%

Time-to-Objective is not computed in this report. No specific named territorial objective with an independently vetted remaining-area figure was available for this assessment; per the Quantitative Methods document's Part VI general rule that every input requires a completed vetting record before use, an unvetted remaining-area figure is not plugged into the formula merely to produce an illustrative number.

Assessment: Likely (per Section 5.2's single-source ceiling) that Russia's rate of territorial advance collapsed to roughly 6% of its prior-year pace in June 2026, consistent with the Analysis Doctrine's Campaign Momentum Model reading of a stalled or heavily-attritted offensive, though confidence in this specific figure is bounded by the absence of a second independently-tracked dataset (Section 5.2).

docs.title: 5.4 Cost Exchange Ratio -- Illustrative, Explicitly Bounded
docs.text:
Per the Quantitative Methods document, Part III. Unit costs below are independent open-source estimates, not vendor- or government-confirmed figures [aggregated open-source defense-cost estimates, Tier 4, Reliability C, Independence Unknown, Vetting: Flag -- aggregated figure, no single traceable original, used only for order-of-magnitude illustration per the Quantitative Methods document's own caveat against self-reported costs].

Shahed-136-type strike drone: C_a ~ $30,000/unit (estimated)
Mobile ground-based fire group interception (guns/EW, typical method
  used against Shahed-type drones): C_d,drone ~ $3,000-5,000/engagement
  (estimated; midpoint $4,000 used below)
Patriot PAC-3 interceptor (used against ballistic missiles): C_d,ballistic
  ~ $4,000,000/unit (estimated)

Drone engagement WCER (illustrative, using this strike's reported
figures: 496 drones launched, 476 intercepted/suppressed):
WCER_drone = (496 x 30,000) / (476 x 4,000) 
           = 14,880,000 / 1,904,000 
           ≈ 7.8

This means the attacker's total estimated drone expenditure this wave
was ~7.8x the defender's total estimated interception cost for the
drone component -- an exchange favoring the DEFENDER, consistent with
open-source reporting that Ukraine deliberately shifted to low-cost
mobile fire groups against Shahed-type drones rather than expensive
guided interceptors, for exactly this reason.

Ballistic missile engagement WCER (illustrative, using 24 Iskander-
class ballistic missiles reported in ISW's munitions breakdown,
assuming for illustration that intercepted ballistic missiles required
one Patriot-class interceptor each -- a simplifying assumption stated
explicitly, since actual interceptor expenditure per intercept was not
independently vetted for this report):
Illustrative per-unit ballistic ER = C_a / C_d = ~3,000,000 / 4,000,000
                                    = 0.75

This is the inverse pattern: the attacker's estimated per-unit
ballistic-missile cost is roughly comparable to, or below, the
defender's per-unit interceptor cost -- an exchange ratio favoring the
ATTACKER on ballistic missiles specifically, unlike the drone
component above.

Assessment: Unlikely-but-not-excluded as precise figures (these rest on an aggregated, F/C-grade cost estimate per the Confidence Assessment Doctrine's F-grade ceiling), but directionally consistent with independently reported Ukrainian doctrine of using low-cost counter-drone methods rather than expensive interceptors against Shahed-type threats specifically because of this exchange asymmetry. The formal Sustainability Threshold (Quantitative Methods, Part III) is not evaluated here in full, since neither side's replenishment rate or allocated budget for these specific munitions/interceptor classes was independently vetted for this report -- this is logged as a Collection Gap (Section 9), not silently assumed.

docs.title: 5.5 Escalation Index
docs.text:
Per the Quantitative Methods document, Part IV, applied to the July 1-2 strike:

ScaleScore         = 72  (record ballistic-missile count for a single
                          Kyiv strike; large total munitions count
                          relative to the recent monthly baseline)
TargetTypeScore    = 68  (critical energy/industrial infrastructure
                          plus substantial reported residential impact)
DomainNoveltyScore = 10  (established strike-package tactic; no new
                          domain or capability class observed)
StatedIntentScore  = 40  (framed by Russia as retaliatory/proportionate,
                          per Section 4.3; but Section 6 identifies
                          scale indicators in tension with a purely
                          proportionate characterization)

EI = (0.30 x 72) + (0.30 x 68) + (0.25 x 10) + (0.15 x 40)
   = 21.6 + 20.4 + 2.5 + 6.0
   = 50.5

Indicative rung: Rung 3 (Degradation), range 41-60, with the ScaleScore and TargetTypeScore subscores (72 and 68) individually approaching the Rung 4 (Theater Expansion, 61-80) threshold. Per the Escalation Doctrine's Part II Rule 3, this is reported as "consistent with Rung 3 (Degradation), with Scale and Target Type components approaching but not reaching Rung 4 (Theater Expansion) thresholds" rather than forced into an unqualified single label. This numeric result is a supplement to, not a replacement for, the indicator-based rung assignment argued qualitatively in Section 6 -- per the Escalation Doctrine's Part II Rule 1 and the Quantitative Methods document's own limitation on this index.

docs.title: 6. Hypothesis Analysis
docs.text: Per the Analysis Doctrine's Hypothesis Engine (Part VIII).

docs.title: Primary Hypothesis
docs.text:
The July 1-2 strike represents continuation and intensification of a sustained degradation campaign against Ukrainian energy and military-industrial infrastructure -- consistent with Escalation Rung 3 (Section 5.5) -- rather than either a strictly proportionate one-off reprisal or a deliberate expansion of the conflict's geographic or domain scope.

Supporting evidence: The strike's stated rationale (retaliation for Ukrainian strikes on Russian oil infrastructure, Section 4.3) fits an established tit-for-tat infrastructure-targeting pattern rather than a novel target set; the domain and general method (missile/drone strike packages) are unchanged from the established campaign baseline (DomainNoveltyScore = 10, Section 5.5); Russia's ground offensive is simultaneously under sustained attritional strain (Section 5.3), consistent with a strategy substituting infrastructure-strike pressure for ground-offensive momentum it currently lacks.

docs.title: Alternative Hypothesis (Seriously Argued)
docs.text:
The strike represents a shift toward coercive signaling that exceeds simple retaliatory proportionality -- using record ballistic-missile density specifically for psychological/political effect on Ukraine's leadership and population, independent of, or beyond, a proportionate response to the triggering Ukrainian strikes.

Supporting evidence: 28 ballistic missiles fired at Kyiv in a single strike is described as a record for the capital [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass] -- proportionate retaliation, per the Escalation Doctrine's Rung 2 definition, implies rough scale-matching to the triggering action, and a record-setting ballistic-missile density is difficult to characterize as scale-matched to a preceding series of Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries. The observed target/impact set -- residential apartment blocks, a hotel, a research institute, an ambulance station [Al Jazeera, Tier 3, Reliability B, Independence High, Vetting: Pass] -- extends meaningfully beyond the "military-industrial facilities and fuel/energy complexes" framing offered by the Russian Ministry of Defense (Section 4.3), a tension noted explicitly rather than resolved by simply adopting either side's framing.

docs.title: Low-Probability Hypothesis (Retained, Not Forgotten)
docs.text: The strike marks the opening move of a deliberate Theater Expansion (Escalation Rung 4) -- a decision to abandon prior restraint on target selection or geographic scope. Retained explicitly per the Hypothesis Engine's instruction not to silently drop a low-probability hypothesis, but currently weakly supported: DomainNoveltyScore is low (10/100, Section 5.5), no new domain or third-party actor is evidenced, and the target set -- while controversial in composition -- remains within the established pattern of this campaign's prior strikes on Kyiv.

docs.title: Disconfirming Evidence Against the Primary Hypothesis
docs.text: Explicitly listed, per the Hypothesis Engine's requirement, even though it did not prevail: the record ballistic-missile density and the breadth of the residential/civilian-adjacent impact set are both in tension with a "routine degradation-campaign continuation" reading, and are the specific evidence points that keep the Alternative Hypothesis live rather than dismissed.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Likely (Confidence Ledger, Section 7) that the Primary Hypothesis better explains the available evidence than the Alternative, primarily because DomainNoveltyScore and the absence of any new target-category precedent weigh heavily against a genuine strategy shift -- but the Alternative Hypothesis is not dismissed, and the specific disconfirming evidence above is retained as an explicit collection-gap trigger (Section 9): a repeat of this ballistic-missile density in the next large-scale strike would materially shift weight toward the Alternative.

docs.title: 7. Confidence Ledger
docs.text: Claim Vetting Result Evidence Strength Source Reliability Corroboration (n) Ceiling(s) Applied Final Confidence >=20 buildings struck directly in Kyiv Pass Strong A 1 None Almost certain / Very likely ~570 total munitions launched (74 missiles/28 ballistic + 496 drones) Pass Strong A 1 (CNBC, B) None Almost certain / Very likely Casualty toll: rising, at least 25-30 killed Pass (multi-source, treated as evolving count per 4.2) Moderate (figures still settling) B/A mix 5+ Stale/settling-evidence note, not a hard ceiling Likely Russian MoD's "military-industrial/fuel-energy" target framing Flag -- incentive disclosed Weak (self-interested characterization, contradicted in part by observed impact set) C 0 Single-source ceiling + unresolved tension w/ 4.4 impact evidence Roughly even chance (leaning against full framing) Russian June 2026 territorial gains = 30.42 km², 1.01 km²/day Pass Strong A 0 Single-source ceiling (Rule 1) Likely Drone-vs-fire-group WCER ≈ 7.8 (favors defender) Flag -- aggregated cost estimate Moderate C (aggregated) 0 F/D-adjacent ceiling on the underlying cost inputs Unlikely, but not excluded (as precise figures; directionally consistent with independent reporting) Primary Hypothesis (sustained degradation campaign) over Alternative Pass (synthesis judgment, not a single sourced fact) Strong (converging indicators) Mixed (A/B primary sources) Multiple Mechanism-absent ceiling not triggered (mechanism articulated in Section 6) Likely

docs.title: 8. Forecast
docs.text:
Per the Forecasting Doctrine. Decision Point: Russian political and military leadership's choice of strike-campaign posture over the next 2-4 weeks, given a ground offensive under sustained attritional strain (Section 5.3) and an unexplained recent reduction in large-scale strike frequency noted by ISW for June 2026 [ISW, Tier 2, Reliability A, Independence High, Vetting: Pass].

Branch 1 -- Sustained Degradation Campaign / No Material Change (Likely). Russia continues the current pattern: periodic large-scale strike waves against energy/military-industrial infrastructure at roughly the recent cadence, combined with continued low-tempo ground gains. Leading indicators: continued logistics/production signals consistent with sustaining current munitions output; no observed change in target-category rhetoric. Lagging indicators: a subsequent large-scale strike wave within the historical interval range, confirming continuity after the fact.

Branch 2 -- Tempo De-escalation / Munitions Conservation (Roughly Even Chance, slightly below Branch 1). Strike frequency and/or scale declines further, consistent with ISW's own noted "unclear reasons" for June 2026's reduced frequency -- possibly production or stockpile constraints. Leading indicators: lengthening intervals between large-scale waves; smaller missile-to-drone ratios in subsequent waves (missiles are more production-constrained than drones). Lagging indicators: explicit confirmation of production/stockpile strain via independent industrial reporting.

Branch 3 -- Further Escalation via New Target Set or Domain (Unlikely). Russia explicitly abandons the "energy/military-industrial" target framing in favor of a new target category, or introduces a new weapons/domain class. Leading indicators: declaratory statements reframing target policy; first confirmed use of a previously-unused capability against Ukraine. Lagging indicators: post-strike confirmation of a genuinely new target category struck.

Branch 4 -- Negotiated De-escalation (Unlikely, contingent on an external diplomatic factor). A ceasefire or strike-pause process gains traction independent of the military trajectory described above. Leading indicators: diplomatic back-channel activity; reciprocal, unilateral pauses in infrastructure strikes by either side. Lagging indicators: a formal agreement text.

Review Trigger: Re-forecast immediately upon (a) a strike wave exceeding the July 1-2 package's ballistic-missile density (would shift weight toward the Alternative Hypothesis in Section 6 and toward Branch 3), or (b) 30 days elapsing without a comparable large-scale wave (would shift weight toward Branch 2).

docs.title: 9. Collection Gaps
docs.text:
• Need independent, imagery-based BDA confirming the specific facility types struck (military-industrial vs. residential vs. mixed-use), to adjudicate the Section 6 tension between Russia's stated target framing and the observed impact set.
  • Need a second, independently-tracked territorial-control dataset for Ukraine to remove the single-source ceiling currently applied to the Section 5.3 momentum figures (Confidence Assessment Doctrine, Part IV, Rule 1).
  • Need confirmation, from Ukrainian procurement or defense-budget reporting, of actual per-unit interception costs for mobile fire-group counter-drone operations, to replace the aggregated, Reliability-C cost estimate used in Section 5.4.
  • Need clarification of ISW's own noted "unclear reasons" for reduced Russian strike frequency in June 2026 -- specifically, independent industrial-production reporting on Shahed-type drone and missile output, to distinguish Branch 2 (production constraint) from a deliberate operational choice.
  • Need a reconciled, authoritative final casualty count from Kyiv city authorities once search-and-rescue operations conclude, to close the evolving-toll range described in Section 4.2.

docs.title: 10.1 Graded Bibliography
docs.text: Source Name Type Domain(s) Tier Reliability Independence Cited In Institute for the Study of War (ISW) Strategic Analysis Military 2 A High 4.1, 4.4, 5.1-5.3, 5.5, 6, 8 CNBC News Military/Political 3 B High 4.1, 4.5 Al Jazeera News Military/Political 3 B High 4.1, 4.3, 6 Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko (official statement) Government (Local) Military 1 B Medium 4.2 PBS NewsHour News Military/Political 3 B High 4.2 Ukraine State Emergency Service (official statement) Government Military 1 B Medium 4.2 Kyiv Independent News Military/Political 3 B Medium 4.2 Russian Ministry of Defense (official statement) Government Military 1 C Low 4.3, 6 Zelensky public statement Government (Head of State) Political/Military 1 B Low-Medium 4.3 NPR News Military/Political 3 B High 4.5 Aggregated open-source defense-cost estimates Aggregator Economic/Military 4 C Unknown 5.4

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
A curated, categorized directory of tools used across OSINT, GEOINT, verification, and adversarial analysis workflows. Each entry includes a brief description and use-case context.

Curation philosophy

This list prioritizes tools that are actively maintained, accessible without paid tiers (or with useful free tiers), and operationally practical. Tools are not ranked — selection depends on your workflow and target.

docs.title: Shodan
docs.text:
In 2015, security researchers queried Shodan for exposed industrial control systems and found active SCADA interfaces — water treatment facilities, power substations, building automation controllers — sitting on public IPs with no authentication. Some ran firmware from 2003. None of them were supposed to be reachable from the internet. All of them were. The operators did not know.

That is Shodan's defining quality. It shows you what is actually running, not what organizations believe they've exposed.

docs.title: What It Actually Is
docs.text:
Google crawls what websites choose to show. Shodan records what services actually respond. The distinction is consequential.

Every 24 to 48 hours, Shodan's distributed scanning infrastructure probes billions of IP addresses across every port it can reach. For each address that responds, it captures the banner — the raw response a service sends when contacted. A web server sends HTTP headers. An SSH daemon announces its version and supported ciphers. A database server may expose its authentication status in its greeting. Shodan captures all of it, parses it, enriches it with geolocation and ASN data, and makes it searchable.

This is not a vulnerability scanner. Shodan does not probe for weaknesses or attempt exploitation. It listens to what services say about themselves, which is often damning enough.

graph TD
    subgraph Google["Google — Content-Driven Indexing"]
        G1[Web Server] -->|"publishes intentional content"| G2[Googlebot Crawler]
        G2 -->|"follows links and parses HTML"| G3[Google Search Index]
    end
    subgraph Shodan["Shodan — Service-Driven Indexing"]
        S1[Global Scan Probes] -->|"hits every IP on every port"| S2[Internet]
        S2 -->|"any response is recorded"| S3[Banner and Service Data]
        S3 -->|"enriched and indexed"| S4[Shodan Search Index]
    end

    style G1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style G2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style G3 fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style S1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style S2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style S3 fill:#111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style S4 fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000

A website can request removal from Google's index. You cannot opt a misconfigured Telnet daemon out of Shodan. If it listens, Shodan finds it.

docs.title: Why It Exists
docs.text:
John Matherly launched Shodan in 2009 with a single goal: give security researchers visibility into the internet's actual attack surface, not the surface as organizations imagined it. What they found was worse than expected. Industrial control systems on public IPs. Medical devices. Routers with default credentials. Database servers with authentication disabled. The scale of unintentionally exposed infrastructure was not a fringe problem — it was systemic.

The tool has since become indispensable across disciplines. Defenders use it to find forgotten assets before attackers do. Threat analysts use it to track adversary infrastructure — command-and-control servers leave persistent fingerprints in Shodan's index. Red teamers use it to build pre-engagement attack surface maps in hours rather than days. Journalists and researchers use it to document exposure at national or sector scale.

The operational principle is the same in every case: the internet is full of services that are exposed without intent and running without oversight. Shodan makes that exposure legible.

docs.title: How Red Teams and Analysts Use It
docs.text:
For a threat analyst building a profile on a known threat actor, Shodan is often the fastest path to uncovering infrastructure the actor hasn't deliberately hidden. Command-and-control frameworks leave recognizable signatures in service banners, SSL certificates, and default configurations. A query matching the SSL certificate fingerprint of a known C2 server can surface a cluster of additional servers the actor operates — many of them not yet attributed anywhere in public reporting.

For a red team operating under authorized scope against an organization, Shodan compresses passive recon from days into hours. Certificate transparency logs combined with Shodan certificate filters enumerate every externally facing service under an organization's certificate authority chain — including test environments, decommissioned applications, and developer boxes that IT stopped tracking years ago.

The most operationally significant thing Shodan reveals is not what organizations know they've exposed. It's what they forgot.

flowchart TD
    A([Target: corp.example.com]) --> B[Shodan Filter\nssl.cert.subject.cn]
    B --> C[Certificate Match\nacross all indexed IPs]
    C --> D1[Port Inventory\nopen services and versions]
    C --> D2[Subdomain Discovery\nstaging, dev, legacy]
    C --> D3[Historical Records\nservice changes over time]
    D1 --> E[Attack Surface Map]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Pivot: IPs to ASN to hosting patterns]
    F --> G([Pre-Engagement Recon Package])

    style A fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style B fill:#111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style C fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style D1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style D2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style D3 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style E fill:#111,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style F fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#e0e0e0
    style G fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000

docs.title: The Dorks
docs.text:
Shodan's query syntax uses search filters. Practitioners call them dorks — borrowed from Google dorking, the practice of using advanced search operators to surface unintentionally exposed content. The principle is identical: precise filters against indexed data reveal exposure that most people assume isn't visible.

ssl.cert.subject.cn:"*.target-corp.com"

Returns every service Shodan has indexed that presented an SSL certificate for any subdomain of the target domain. Add http.status:200 to limit results to live web services, or port:3389 to find Remote Desktop servers running under the corporate certificate chain. This single filter is the fastest way to enumerate an organization's externally visible infrastructure without touching any of their systems. It works because TLS certificates are served publicly by design — the certificate reveals the subdomain whether or not the subdomain appears in DNS.

product:"Cobalt Strike Beacon" port:443

Cobalt Strike is a commercial adversary simulation framework that has been widely leaked and is now among the most common command-and-control platforms used by ransomware operators and nation-state actors. When a Cobalt Strike server uses default settings or weak OPSEC, Shodan fingerprints it by service banner characteristics. Threat intelligence teams use this query to locate active C2 infrastructure. Defenders use it to verify their own red team cleaned up after an engagement. It also surfaces previously untracked infrastructure from tracked actors when cross-referenced against known IP ranges or ASNs.

port:9200 product:"Elasticsearch" country:"US" org:"Amazon"

Elasticsearch databases exposed on port 9200 without authentication have been the source of dozens of major breach disclosures over the past five years — billions of records, most of them never intentionally made public. Narrowing by organization to Amazon Web Services isolates databases running on AWS with misconfigured security groups, which is disproportionately common. This filter does not access any database. It identifies which ones are publicly reachable and how many exist. The numbers are consistently higher than they should be.

has_screenshot:true http.title:"Login" port:3389

Shodan captures screenshots of HTTP interfaces and RDP login screens during its scanning cycles. This filter returns Remote Desktop services with visible login prompts that are publicly addressable. For red team pre-engagement recon, this identifies machines running RDP on public IPs before any target system is contacted. The has_screenshot:true filter generalizes well — applied to other ports and service types, it enables rapid visual triage of exposed interfaces without active probing.

graph TD
    A[Shodan Dorks] --> B1["ssl.cert.subject.cn\nCertificate-Based Asset Discovery"]
    A --> B2["product: Cobalt Strike Beacon\nAdversary Infrastructure Detection"]
    A --> B3["port:9200 Elasticsearch\nExposed Database Enumeration"]
    A --> B4["has_screenshot:true\nVisual Interface Recon"]

    B1 --> C1([Enumerate org services\nwithout touching systems])
    B2 --> C2([Map threat actor\nC2 cluster])
    B3 --> C3([Identify unauthenticated\ndatabases at scale])
    B4 --> C4([Visual triage of\nexposed interfaces])

    style A fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style B2 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style B3 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style B4 fill:#1a1a1a,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#e0e0e0
    style C1 fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#aaa
    style C2 fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#aaa
    style C3 fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#aaa
    style C4 fill:#111,stroke:#555,color:#aaa

docs.title: Access and the API
docs.text:
Shodan is not free for serious use. The free tier allows basic searches with limited results. A membership tier ($49/year at time of writing) unlocks full result pagination, API access, and historical banner data. Academic researchers can apply for elevated access at no cost. Enterprise plans add continuous monitoring, alerts, and bulk export.

The API is where Shodan becomes operationally useful. The Python SDK and command-line client enable integration into automated pipelines — monitoring an organization's IP ranges for new services that appear overnight, alerting when a tracked threat actor's certificate fingerprint appears on a new IP, or pulling regular snapshots of an external attack surface for diff-based analysis. A query that takes thirty seconds manually runs unattended and feeds directly into a case management workflow.

Querying is not accessing

Searching Shodan queries its index of passively observed public data — no target system is contacted. However, acting on what you find is a different matter. Accessing an unauthenticated database, testing credentials against an exposed service, or connecting to a system you do not own or have authorization for is illegal in most jurisdictions regardless of how the exposure occurred. The legal boundary is between passive observation and active interaction. Know where it is before you cross it.

For live Shodan analysis, infrastructure tracking walkthroughs, and adversary C2 hunting breakdowns, follow @tactical_osint on Telegram.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Verification is the process of confirming that a piece of information, image, or video is authentic and accurately represents what it claims. This section covers technical and analytical methods for verifying open-source media.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Topic Description Image Verification Reverse search, metadata analysis, and forensic indicators Video Verification Frame analysis, geolocation from footage, and deepfake detection Source Credibility Frameworks for evaluating source reliability and bias
